coredata.dc:title: Mechanically robust, flexible, conductive, and anti-freezing hydrogels reinforced by cellulose of wood skeleton

coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Luzhen
You, Muqiu

Zhou, Jing

Li, Junshuai
Chen, Chuchu
coredata.dc:description: Hydrogels are soft and wet materials, but their applications are always limited by insufficient mechanical strength and toughness, and they are prone to freezing at low temperatures. In this study, we introduced an eco-friendly approach to developing wood-based hydrogels reinforced by the naturally aligned wood skeleton (WS) through the Hofmeister effect. The resulting wood-based composite hydrogels exhibited a high tensile strength of 20 MPa and a strain of 35 % in the longitudinal direction. This impressive mechanical performance was primarily due to densely packed hydrogen bonding, physical entanglements, and van der Waals forces between the cellulose of WS, polyacrylamide (PAM), and poly(vinyl alcohol) (PVA) chains during polymerization. Notably, the polymerization was induced using wood carbon dots as initiators, imparting additional fluorescence features to the hydrogels. Afterward, by incorporating a metal salt (sodium chloride), the developed wood-based hydrogels maintained high conductivity (3.0 S/m) and mechanical properties even under low-temperature conditions (−20 °C). Moreover, the conductive hydrogels exhibited multifunctional sensing capabilities, including strain, temperature, and ultraviolet (UV) irradiation detection, making them highly suitable for applications in human motion monitoring and healthcare, particularly under harsh environmental conditions.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Wood-based hydrogels
Anti-freezing
Mechanical strength
Polyacrylamide (PAM)
Poly(vinyl alcohol) (PVA)

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagram of preparation, formation mechanism and application of the developed PAM/PVA-NaCl/WS hydrogels.
FE-SEM cross-sectional micrographs of (a), (e) balsa wood; (b), (f) wood skeleton; (c), (g) PAM/PVA/WS; (d), (h) PAM/PVA-NaCl15/WS;

FTIR spectra of the wood-based hydrogel: (a) Wood, Wood Skeleton, PAM/PVA/WS; (b) PAM/PVA-NaCl/WS; (c) XRD spectrum of PAM/PVA-NaCl/WS; (d) XRD spectrum of W-CQDs; (e) UV–vis absorption spectra of PAM/PVA-NaCl15/WS hydrogel at 360 nm; (f) fluorescence spectra of PAM/PVA-NaCl15/WS at 335 nm;

Tensile stress-strain curves of the PAM/PVA-NaCl/WS hydrogel samples, (a) L-direction, (b) R-direction; (c) cycling curves of PAM/PVA-NaCl15/WS hydrogel samples; (d) photographs of PAM/PVA-NaCl15/WS hydrogel samples.

Anti-freezing performance of the wood-based hydrogels (a) Photographs of the wood-based hydrogels in different temperature; (b) DSC spectrum of wood-based hydrogels; (c) tensile stress-strain curves of the PAM/PVA-NaCl15/WS in different temperature; (d) conductivity of the PAM/PVA-NaCl/WS in different temperature; (e), (f) photo of the PAM/PVA-NaCl15/WS.

Relative current changes of wood-based hydrogels as wearable sensors for monitoring human motion and pressure. (a) A schematic diagram of the assembly into flexible sensors; (b), (c) relative current changes with stress sensing responses; (d) finger bending in different angles; (e) wrist bending; (f) finger bending; (g) knee bending; (h), (i) morse code and relative current changes of “PAM”;

Relative current changes of wood-based hydrogel as wearable sensors for health monitoring. Relative current changes with (a) swallow and cough response; (b) pulse; (c) temperature; (d) UV illumination (365 nm, 36 W);

head.ce:title: Mechanically robust, flexible, conductive, and anti-freezing hydrogels reinforced by cellulose of wood skeleton

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Materials Science and Engineering
Jiangsu Co-Innovation Center of Efficient Processing and Utilization of Forest Resources

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Hydrogels are soft and wet materials, but their applications are always limited by insufficient mechanical strength and toughness, and they are prone to freezing at low temperatures. In this study, we introduced an eco-friendly approach to developing wood-based hydrogels reinforced by the naturally aligned wood skeleton (WS) through the Hofmeister effect. The resulting wood-based composite hydrogels exhibited a high tensile strength of 20 MPa and a strain of 35 % in the longitudinal direction. This impressive mechanical performance was primarily due to densely packed hydrogen bonding, physical entanglements, and van der Waals forces between the cellulose of WS, polyacrylamide (PAM), and poly(vinyl alcohol) (PVA) chains during polymerization. Notably, the polymerization was induced using wood carbon dots as initiators, imparting additional fluorescence features to the hydrogels. Afterward, by incorporating a metal salt (sodium chloride), the developed wood-based hydrogels maintained high conductivity (3.0 S/m) and mechanical properties even under low-temperature conditions (−20 °C). Moreover, the conductive hydrogels exhibited multifunctional sensing capabilities, including strain, temperature, and ultraviolet (UV) irradiation detection, making them highly suitable for applications in human motion monitoring and healthcare, particularly under harsh environmental conditions.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data are available from the authors upon reasonable request.

ce:section.ce:para: With the continuous advancement of technology, hydrogel-based materials have gained significant attention for potential applications in the Internet of Things (IoT) due to their lightweight, high flexibility, and stretchability [1–3]. However, the high water content of hydrogels weakens their intramolecular and intermolecular chain interactions, leading to poor mechanical properties [4,5]. Water molecules within hydrogels can be categorized based on their interaction strength with the polymeric network as “bound water” and “free water” [6]. The presence of “free water” causes hydrogels to be freezed at low temperatures (around −10 °C), resulting in environmental instability and a loss of flexibility and conductivity in harsh environments [7]. Despite the development of toughening strategies such as directed freeze-casting [8–10], double-network structure design [11,12], and multiple hydrogen bonding synergy [13,14], achieving both high mechanical properties (simultaneously soft and strong) and environmental tolerance in hydrogels remains challenging.

Wood, a renewable resource with neatly arranged microchannels, interconnected pore networks, and excellent mechanical properties, has shown promise in various applications [15–20]. Recently, methods to prepare anisotropic hydrogels using wood skeleton (WS) as the template with oriented structures have been reported to effectively improve the mechanical performance [5,21,22]. For example, lignin removal from wood, followed by filling the resulting WS with hydrophilic polymers such as polyacrylamide (PAM) and poly(vinyl alcohol) (PVA), has enabled the production of hydrogels with superior strength [23]. These wood-based hydrogels can achieve or exceed a breaking strength of >10 MPa due to the aligned cellulose fibers in WS as well as the interactions between wood and polymers [24–27]. However, the stiff crystalline structure of cellulose makes it challenging to transform into flexible and soft wood-based hydrogels, limiting their applications in wearable sensors. Furthermore, although in-situ polymerization of monomers is a typical strategy for preparing hydrogels, the use of toxic photoinitiators often compromises biocompatibility and environmental sustainability [28].

The solubility of hydrophilic polymers in aqueous systems is influenced by salt ions, an effect known as the Hofmeister effect [29]. Research has explored the relationship between various ions and polymer solubility, making it possible to prepare hydrogels with tunable mechanical properties. For instance, Wu et al. [30] reported a strategy to reversibly alter the mechanical properties of hydrogels by tuning the aggregation states of polymer chains (with ions) based on the Hofmeister effect. They used ultratough PVA hydrogel as an example, which achieved a tensile strength of 15 ± 1 MPa. Although there have been advancements in hydrogels with good mechanical performance, various difficulties must be overcome to expand their practical applications. For example, hydrogel-based devices must have long-term stability during open-air operation while maintaining mechanical deformability and electrical conductivity in cold environments. [31]. However, the freezing of water significantly weakens the mechanical and electrical properties of hydrogels, leading to poor environmental stability and limiting their applications [32,33].

Recently, photoinitiators based on carbon dots and carbon nitrides have been developed from wood due to their eco-friendliness and high biocompatibility [34–36]. Herein, we reported a green strategy using nitrogen-doped wood carbon dots as a photoinitiator to initiate grafting polymerization via ultraviolet (UV) irradiation, resulting in mechanically strong and flexible PAM/PVA-NaCl/WS hydrogels. Specifically, the developed wood-based hydrogels exhibited high strength (∼20 MPa), flexibility (strain: ∼35 %), toughness, conductivity, and anti-freezing properties (−20 °C). These combined merits make the hydrogels promising candidates for applications in smart clothing, wearables, and soft robotics, particularly under extreme conditions.

Balsa wood (Ochroma pyramidale) was sliced in the direction of its growth with a dimension of 40 × 40 × 1 mm3 (longitudinal × radial × tangential, L × R × T). Sodium hydroxide (NaOH, ≥96 %), sodium chlorite (NaClO2, 80 %), Acrylamide (AM, 99 %), Polyvinyl alcohol (PVA, 1799 Mw ≈ 79,000 g/mol), N, N′-methylene bisacrylamide (MBA, 99 %), Urea (CH4N2O, 99 %), and the ethanol were acquired from Shanghai Aladdin Biological Technology Co., Ltd.

To synthesize nitrogen-doped Balsa carbon quantum dots (W-CQDs), 1.25 g of Balsa wood powder was used as the carbon source and dispersed in 50 mL of water. Subsequently, 1.25 g of urea was added, and the mixture was stirred thoroughly to ensure complete homogenization. The resulting solution was transferred to a 100 mL Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave, which was then sealed and heated in an oven at 180 °C for 10 h. After cooling to room temperature, the suspension was collected and centrifuged at 1000 ×g for 10 min to remove larger particulates. The supernatant was further purified through ultrafiltration via dialysis (1 kDa membrane) for 48 h, gaining the final W-CQDs.

The delignification of wood slices was performed using a 2 wt% sodium chlorite (NaClO₂) solution in an acetate-buffered medium, maintained at pH 4.6 and heated to boiling point for 2 h. Following delignification, the treated wood samples were immersed in a 15 wt% sodium hydroxide (NaOH) solution at 40 °C for an additional 2 h. The final product was designated as the “wood skeleton” (WS).

The solution preparation involved dissolving 0.054 g of N,N′-methylenebisacrylamide (MBA) (1 wt% of acrylamide, AM) and 0.2 g of W-CQDs in 10.8 g of H2O. This solution was then combined with the 0.6 g of polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) and 5.4 g of AM. Subsequently, sodium chloride (NaCl) was added in varying amounts (0 g, 0.58 g, 1.22 g, and 1.94 g), corresponding to 0 wt%, 5 wt%, 10 wt%, and 15 wt% of the prepolymer solution, respectively.

For the fabrication of PAM/PVA-NaCl/WS hydrogels, the WS was fully submerged in the precursor solution at ambient conditions for 3 h. After complete immersion, the wood sample was placed between two glass slides and secured using a silicone spacer (1 mm thickness) to maintain uniformity during polymerization. The assembly was then exposed to ultraviolet (UV) irradiation using a UV lamp (36 W, peak emission wavelength ∼ 365 nm) for 2 h to initiate polymerization. The resulting hydrogel materials were designated as PAM/PVA-NaClₙ/WS hydrogels, where ‘n’ represents the specific NaCl ratios of 5, 10, or 15 wt%.

The microstructure of the wood-based hydrogels were analyzed using a field emission scanning electron microscope (FE-SEM, Hitachi Regulus 8100, Japan) at an operating voltage of 1.5 kV after freeze-drying.

The fluorescence spectra of the hydrogel samples were recorded using a spectrofluorometer (LS 55, PerkinElmer, USA). The surface chemical characteristics of the samples were analyzed using a Nicolet iS10 Fourier-transform infrared (FTIR) spectrometer (Thermo Scientific, USA) equipped with an Attenuated Total Reflection (ATR) system, as well as UV–Vis spectroscopy (Thermo Scientific, USA).

The PAM structure was examined using a Raman spectrometer (HORIBA Xplora Plus, HORIBA, France). X-ray diffraction (XRD) measurements were performed on an Ultima IV X-ray diffractometer (Rigaku, Japan) using Cu Kα radiation (λ = 0.154 nm) at 40 kV and 40 mA.

The thermal behavior of the hydrogels was investigated using a differential scanning calorimeter (DSC 25, TA Instruments, USA). A sample (∼10 mg) was immediately sealed in an aluminum DSC pan to prevent variations in the solvent fraction and then scanned under a nitrogen atmosphere from −80 °C to 20 °C at a heating rate of 5 °C/min.

Mechanical properties were evaluated on samples using a universal mechanical testing machine at a room temperature (20 ± 1 °C) and relative humidity (50 ± 1 % RH). A 100 N force sensor was employed for all tests (SANS, Shenzhen Co. Ltd., China), and the tensile speed was set to 50 mm/min.

The fabricated hydrogel specimens were immersed in deionized water at ambient temperature and allowed to equilibrate until no further change in mass was observed. The initial and final masses of the hydrogels in their fully swollen state were recorded, and the swelling ratio was subsequently calculated. Additionally, the water content of each hydrogel sample was quantitatively determined by comparing its weight after dehydration with its initial mass.

The relative current of the PAM/PVA-NaClₙ/WS hydrogel was measured using an electrochemical workstation (CHI600E, Shanghai Chenhua Instrument Co., Ltd., China). To evaluate the sensing performance, the PAM/PVA-NaCl15/WS hydrogel sensor was attached to human skin or room models and subjected to various stimuli. The current dynamic response of the sensor was recorded in I-t mode under an applied voltage of 2 V.

The mechanical and anti-freezing properties of wood-based hydrogels should be improved to resist potentially harsh conditions and enable their application as flexible sensors in various scenarios. In this study, the lignin and hemicellulose components in natural wood samples were first extracted to release the strong bonds between cellulose fibers while preserving the naturally aligned structure of the WS. The polymerization was then initiated by nitrogen-doped wood carbon dots under UV irradiation, which served as a green photoinitiator. Subsequently, PAM and PVA were introduced into the WS along with sodium chloride (NaCl), forming PAM/PVA-NaCl/WS hydrogels (Fig. 1 ).

Specifically, UV irradiation promoted favorable interfacial interactions between PAM and WS through a grafting reaction, constructing a robust WS-reinforced polymer network [37]. Simultaneously, the Hofmeister effect, induced by NaCl, facilitated the folding and precipitation of PVA chains, forming strong hydrogen bonds and physical entanglements between the WS and polymer chains (PAM and PVA). This dual reinforcement significantly enhanced the mechanical properties of the composite hydrogels, while the presence of the metal salt also endowed the material with excellent anti-freezing properties and high conductivity.

The synergistic combination of the aligned WS, hydrogen bonding, physical entanglements, and the Hofmeister effect resulted in a composite hydrogel with integrated mechanical strength, flexibility, and environmental stability. This straightforward and eco-friendly design approach not only addressed the limitations of mechanical weakness for the hydrogels but also make them suitable for wide application in sustainable technologies.

The natural wood exhibits a three-dimensional interconnected structure characterized by extensive porosity with large vessels (diameter: ∼13.2 to 58.4 μm) and tiny tracheid channels (diameter: ∼3.4 to 12.3 μm), as shown in Fig. 2a and e. After removing the matrix components (lignin and hemicellulose) from the wood, the cell walls appeared to be thinner, decreasing from 2.3 to 0.72 μm, while the honeycomb structure was well-preserved (Fig. 2b and f). The porous structures facilitated the polymer filling and ion transport in the hydrogels. Upon filling the WS with PAM and PVA, it was evident that all the pores were completely filled without any noticeable interfacial gaps (Fig. 2c and g). The PAM/PVA-NaCl15/WS hydrogels exhibited a uniform and dense structure within the WS (Fig. 2d and h). This was likely due to the increased physical entanglement of PVA polymer chains, which enhanced the mechanical properties, consistent with the results from mechanical testing.

Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) analysis was performed to investigate the chemical evolution during the formation of PAM/PVA-NaCl/WS hydrogels. As shown in Fig. 3a, the intensity of peaks corresponding to CO and CO stretching vibrations decreased after the removal of lignin and hemicellulose from the wood, confirming the reduction of matrix components [38]. The band at approximately 1031 cm−1, attributable to the C-O-C pyranose ring stretching in cellulose, indicated that PAM (FT-IR and Raman spectroscopy analyses of PAM are shown in Fig. S2) was grafted from the cellulose of the WS after UV irradiation [39]. The introduction of PVA caused a shift of the -OH vibrational absorption peak from 2930 (WS) to 2934 cm−1 (PAM/PVA/WS), demonstrating the formation of hydrogen bonds between PVA and WS [40]. The -OH tensile vibration peak of PVA in PAM/PVA/WS shifted from 3319 to 3324 cm−1, confirming the interactions of hydrogen bonds between PVA and PAM [41]. Moreover, the hydrogen bonding between PVA and PAM chains was enhanced with the increasing salt concentration, as evidenced by the shift of the -OH stretch peak from 3324 (in PAM/PVA-NaCl5/WS) to 3336 cm−1 (in PAM/PVA-NaCl15/WS) (Fig. 3b) [42].

X-ray diffraction (XRD) was employed to investigate the changes in the crystallinity of PAM/PVA-NaCl/WS hydrogels with varying NaCl concentrations (Fig. 3c). The diffraction intensity at 2θ of 19.4° increased with higher NaCl contents. This was attributed to the Hofmeister effect, which promoted the crystallization of PAM/PVA-NaCl/WS. Moreover, the diffraction peaks observed at 2θ of 28, 32, 45, and 57° could be indexed to the crystalline planes of NaCl. These results indicated that the crystalline structure of the composite hydrogels was enhanced by the presence of inorganic salts, independent of chemical crosslinking processes [43,44].

The polymerization of PAM/PVA-NaCl/WS, initiated by nitrogen-doped wood-derived carbon dots (W-CQDs), was further investigated (Fig. 3d-f). The broad peak (Fig. 3d) signified the amorphous nature of W-CQDs, related to the arrangement of the graphitic lattice, which contained a significant amount of disordered carbon atoms. The optical absorption characteristics of the hydrogels were further analyzed by recording their UV–visible (UV–vis) absorbance spectrum (Fig. 3e). The absorbance peak at 290 nm, associated with the π–π* transition of aromatic CC bonds within the localized sp2-conjugated clusters at the center, indicated that the hydrogels effectively absorbed UV light, leading to luminescence [45]. Based on this information, the fluorescence spectra of the hydrogel were tested, and a green-blue color was observed for the developed hydrogels upon exposure to 365 nm radiation (Fig. S1). These results demonstrated that the incorporated W-CQDs, not only acted as initiators to induce the polymerization but also imparted fluorescence performance to the hydrogels under UV light excitation. The fluorescence emitted from the wood-based hydrogels was excitation-dependent in the 380–450 nm band (Fig. 3f) [46]. The excitation-dependent fluorescence behavior can be attributed to the presence of multiple functional groups, composed of C, N, and O atoms, on the surface of the W-CQDs. The functional groups created diverse surface states with varying energy levels and emissive traps, leading to the observed fluorescence variations [47].

The inherent anisotropy of natural wood caused significant differences in the mechanical properties of the developed wood-based hydrogels along the radial (R) and longitudinal (L) directions. As shown in Fig. 4a, the tensile strength of the hydrogels gradually increased with the rise in the NaCl concentration. The PAM/PVA-NaCl15/WS exhibited a tensile strength of >20 MPa along the L direction. Additionally, its elongation at break exceeded 35 %, much higher than most of the reported wood hydrogels (<20 %), indicating a unique combination of softness and stretchability. The exceptional mechanical performance was primarily attributed to the crystalline domains of PVA chains and the densely packed hydrogen bonds between the polymer chains and the WS within the hydrogel networks.

The anisotropic mechanical properties of the hydrogel were further investigated in the R direction (Fig. 4b). Results revealed that all hydrogels exhibited significantly lower tensile strength (<1.6 MPa) and a corresponding strain of 50 %. This variation in mechanical properties can be attributed to the anisotropic structure of the WS. Specifically, the tensile strength of PAM/PVA-NaCl/WS in the L direction depended on the disruption of rigid fiber chains, the covalent bonds between cellulose and PAM chains, and the numerous hydrogen bonds. In contrast, stretching the hydrogels along the R direction did not require the destruction of rigid fibers, leading to the observed differences in mechanical performance.

Cyclic tensile tests were conducted to assess the toughness (Fig. S3) of PAM/PVA-NaCl/WS, and the results are depicted in Fig. 4c. Distinct hysteresis loops were observed in the loading-unloading curves under varying strains, demonstrating significant energy dissipation during cyclic deformation. This behavior originated from the dynamic reorganization of hydrogen bonds and physical entanglements within the hydrogel network, which reversibly broke and reformed to dissipate energy while maintaining structural integrity. Furthermore, the PAM/PVA-NaCl15/WS was knotted and stretched into a tie along the longitudinal direction without any damage, demonstrating super-flexibility (Fig. 4d).

Anti-freezing performance is one of the critical challenges for hydrogel materials used in low-temperature conditions. Therefore, the developed wood-based hydrogels were tested at −20 °C to evaluate their anti-freezing properties (Fig. 5a). Obviously, the PAM/PVA/WS hydrogel (without NaCl) froze into an ice-like solid and fractured upon twisting. In contrast, the PAM/PVA-NaCl15/WS hydrogel remained flexible and intact under the same conditions. Differential scanning calorimetry (DSC) was further performed on the wood-based hydrogels to explore their environmental tolerance (from −80 to 20 °C, Fig. 5b). We found that the introduction of electrolyte salt (NaCl) could inhibit ice formation in the hydrogels, shifting the melting point from −17 (PAM/PVA/WS) to −32 °C (PAM/PVA-NaCl15/WS), indicating improved anti-freezing properties.

Tensile tests were further conducted to demonstrate the anti-freezing performance of PAM/PVA-NaCl15/WS. As shown in Fig. 5c, the hydrogel gradually hardened with the decreased temperature but still exhibited a high strain of around 20 % even at −30 °C. The anisotropic conductivity of the hydrogels at different temperatures was also studied (Fig. 5d). Due to the inherent anisotropy of the WS, all the hydrogels showed a much higher conductivity in the L direction than that of the R direction, reaching 3.0 S/m at −20 °C with 15 % NaCl. When integrated into a circuit as a conductive wire, the PAM/PVA-NaCl15/WS hydrogel maintained consistent light-emitting diode (LED) brightness at −20 °C (Fig. 5e and f), demonstrating the stability as a conductive material under freezing conditions. These observations indicated that the developed PAM/PVA-NaCl/WS hydrogels could be an effective anti-freezing sensing component for wearable devices in cold environments.

Due to the advantages of anti-freezing capability, extraordinary ionic conductivity, and superior mechanical properties, the PAM/PVA-NaCl15/WS was designed as a strain sensor and connected with a universal stretching machine to detect the relative current at −20 °C (Fig. 6a). We applied a strain of 25 % to the hydrogel sensor for 50 cycles to assess its stability and durability. The curve of the relative current change remained highly consistent across all cycles, with smooth and reproducible current variations during deformation (Fig. 6b and c).

The PAM/PVA-NaCl15/WS hydrogel was attached to various human joints, including fingers, wrists, and knees, to explore its potential as a wearable sensor (Fig. 6d-g) and detect motion signals. The joints of the body exhibited varying degrees of maximum flexion, leading to distinct feedback signals. For example, as shown in Fig. 6d, the bending angles of the fingers were approximately 15, 45, and 90°, with the corresponding ∆I/I₀ values of 17.8 %, 22.3 %, and 31.2 %, respectively. The bending angle of the wrist (Fig. 6e) was measured at <90°, resulting in a corresponding ∆I/I₀ value of 21.2 %. The movement signals at the finger (Fig. 6f) and knee (Fig. 6g) were also monitored. The relative current signals from the sensors confirmed the ability to differentiate between various motions at low temperatures.

Additionally, the hydrogel sensor demonstrated potential for the transmission of encrypted information using Morse code, where letters were represented by combinations of dots (short bends) and lines (long bends, Fig. 6h). By correlating finger bending duration with electrical signals, words such as “PAM” were successfully encoded and decoded (Fig. 6i). This capability highlights the potential of anti-freezing hydrogel sensors for advanced applications in secure communication and wearable technology.

With the increasing focus on health issues driven by societal advancements and progress in medical technology, flexible wearable technologies have emerged as a promising tool for personalized diagnosis and treatment. Herein, PAM/PVA-NaCl15/WS sensors were employed to detect subtle physiological changes, such as swallowing and pulse, as well as to monitor skin temperature and UV exposure. As shown in Fig. 7a, the sensor detected a small current signal during normal swallowing. However, it exhibited a pronounced peak during coughing, demonstrating the ability to accurately distinguish different physiological responses. Additionally, the sensor could monitor blood pulse when attached to the wrist of a volunteer, recording a pulse rate of 93 beats per minute (Fig. 7b), which was within the healthy adult range of 60 to 100 beats per minute [48].

Moreover, the PAM/PVA-NaCl15/WS sensors showed excellent performance in temperature variation tests (Fig. 7c). When exposed to repeated temperature changes between 36 and 38 °C, the sensors produced rapid, stable, and repeatable current signals, highlighting their potential for continuous body temperature monitoring and real-time feedback. Furthermore, the UV detection capability of the sensors was evaluated under UV light (wavelength: 365 nm; irradiance: 289 μW/cm2). The relative current signals of the sensor increased significantly upon UV exposure due to the photoresponsive properties of wood-derived carbon quantum dots (W-CQDs, Fig. 7d). This enabled effective monitoring of UV intensity through current changes. In summary, PAM/PVA-NaCl15/WS hydrogel sensors showed promise in various health monitoring applications, including detecting physiological changes, body temperature, and UV exposure.

This study synthesized conductive, fluorescent, and flexible wood-based hydrogels with exceptional mechanical and anti-freezing properties through UV-induced polymerization, using wood-derived carbon dots as green photoinitiators. The synthesis process was initiated with the grafting of PAM from the aligned cellulose, followed by the physical entanglement of polymer chains (PAM and PVA) with the WS through hydrogen bonding. Afterward, the folding and precipitation of PVA chains, driven by the Hofmeister effect (under NaCl treatment), further enhanced the mechanical properties of the hydrogels. As a result, the developed PAM/PVA-NaCl15/WS hydrogels exhibited remarkable flexibility, achieving a high tensile strength of 20 MPa and a strain of 35 % in the longitudinal direction. Furthermore, these wood-based hydrogels demonstrated high conductivity (3.4 S/m) and anti-freezing capabilities (−32 °C). Moreover, they could be assembled into multifunctional sensors, showing responsiveness to deformation, temperature changes, UV irradiation, and other pressure variations under extreme conditions. The sustainable strategy offers valuable insights for the development of wood-based composites, paving the way for advanced applications in health monitoring, electronic skins, and wearable devices.

Luzhen Wang: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Visualization, Software, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Muqiu You: Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Software, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Jinhao Xu: Writing – review & editing, Software, Methodology. Jing Zhou: Software, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Yongcan Jin: Resources, Project administration. Dagang Li: Supervision, Resources, Project administration. Zhaoyang Xu: Validation, Supervision, Resources, Project administration. Junshuai Li: Validation, Supervision, Resources. Chuchu Chen: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Supervision, Resources, Project administration, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors would like to thank the financial support from National Natural Science Foundation of China (32471792); The 14th China Special Postdoctoral Science Foundation (2021T140329); China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (2020M671506) and Postdoctoral Scientific Research Grant Program of Jiangsu Province (2020Z027) for financial supports.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ijbiomac.2025.142049.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Entanglement in smart hydrogels: fast response time, anti-freezing and anti-drying

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Feng, S. Wang, Y. Li, W. Ma, G. Zhang, M. Yang, H. Li, Y. Yang, Y. Long, Entanglement in smart hydrogels: fast response time, anti-freezing and anti-drying, Advanced Functional Materials 33(21) (2023) 2211027.
Y. S. Zhang, A. Khademhosseini, Advances in engineering hydrogels, Science 356(6337) (2017) eaaf3627.
Q. F. Guan, H. B. Yang, Z. M. Han, Z. C. Ling, C. H. Yin, K. P. Yang, Y. X. Zhao, S. H. Yu, Sustainable cellulose-nanofiber-based hydrogels, ACS Nano 15(5) (2021) 7889-7898.
